meta.model_note: Use claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 for fallback API calls

responses.response: HV offers 23 optional subjects: Art & Design, Business Studies, Financial Education (CeFE), Child Development, Combined Portuguese/Spanish, Computer Science, Design Technology, Food & Nutrition, French, Geography, History, ICT, Motor Vehicle Studies, Music BTEC, Music GCSE, Performing Arts BTEC, Photography, Psychology, Religious Studies, Spanish, Sport BTEC, Construction (Highlands), Hair & Beauty (Highlands). You pick 3 — plus core English, Maths, and Science.

You choose 3 options. Choice 1 must be from: Computer Science, French, Geography, History, Psychology, Religious Studies, Spanish, or Combined Portuguese/Spanish. Choices 2 and 3 are free from the full list. Also pick 2 reserve subjects. Submit via studentoptions.co/hautevallee using your login.
The deadline for the current 2025 cycle was 8pm on Friday 7 March 2025. If you've missed it, contact the school directly on 01534 736524 or enquiries@hv.sch.je.
Before the deadline you can log back into the platform and update your choices. After the deadline, contact the school directly — speak to your Head of Year or the school office.
All students must take: IGCSE English Language, IGCSE English Literature, IGCSE Mathematics, and Triple Science (Biology, Chemistry, Physics). Plus core PE. These are on top of your 3 chosen options.

The EBacc is a combination of GCSEs covering English, Maths, Science, a language, and a humanity (History or Geography). HV encourages students to follow this route — which is why Choice 1 must be from the humanities/languages group.

HV starts GCSEs in Year 8 so students study over 3 years instead of the usual 2. This reduces pressure, improves results, and allows exams to be sat when students are ready — not just at end of Year 11.

keywords: which is better btec or gcse
responses.response: GCSEs are mostly exam-based and graded 1-9. BTECs are coursework and project-based, graded Pass/Merit/Distinction (equivalent to GCSE grades). BTECs suit practical learners who prefer continuous assessment over final exams.

HV offers 3 vocational courses with Highlands College: Construction, Hair & Beauty, and Motor Vehicle Studies. You can only pick ONE of these. Year 9 is school-based; Years 10-11 involve one afternoon per week at Highlands College.
Subjects with no traditional written exam: Art & Design, Photography, Performing Arts BTEC, Music BTEC. Mostly practical with minimal writing: Construction, Hair & Beauty, Motor Vehicle, Sport BTEC.

High coursework subjects: Art (60% portfolio), Photography (60% portfolio), Child Development (60% NEA), Design Technology (50% project), Food & Nutrition (50% coursework), English Language (50% coursework).

There’s no single “easiest” subject — it depends on your child’s strengths! But if they prefer less exam pressure: Art, Photography, Performing Arts BTEC, and Music BTEC have no written exams. Child Development (60% coursework), Design Technology (50% coursework), and Food & Nutrition (50% coursework) spread the assessment across the year. Construction, Hair & Beauty, and Motor Vehicle are highly practical with minimal written work. The best choice is one your child enjoys — motivation matters more than difficulty.
Art & Design (AQA) is 100% practical — no written exam. Portfolio 60% + 10-hour controlled exam piece 40%. Great for creative, independent students. Course leader: Mrs Boccone (s.boccone@hv.sch.je).

Business Studies (Edexcel) is 100% exam — two 1hr 45min papers covering starting and growing a business. Good for students interested in entrepreneurship and real-world contexts. Leader: Miss Dimaro (s.dimaro@hv.sch.je).

The Certificate in Financial Education (CeFE) is a Level 2 Technical Award covering public finance, budgeting, and employability. Not a traditional GCSE but Level 2 equivalent. Complements Maths and Business. Leader: Miss Dimaro (s.dimaro@hv.sch.je).

Child Development (OCR Cambridge National) — 40% exam, 60% coursework. Covers child health, nutrition, and development from birth to 5 years. Good for careers in early years, midwifery, or childcare. Leader: Ms Shanklyn (v.shanklyn@hv.sch.je).

Computer Science (Edexcel 1CP2) has two papers: theory and a practical Python 3 programming exam. Good for logical thinkers strong in Maths. Must be chosen as Choice 1. Leader: Miss Dimaro (s.dimaro@hv.sch.je).
Design Technology (IGCSE) — 50% coursework project, 50% written papers (Product Design + Resistant Materials). Practical, creative and technical. Leader: Mr Browne (S.Browne@hv.sch.je).

Food Preparation & Nutrition (AQA) — 50% written exam, 50% practical coursework (food investigation + food prep assessment). Good for students interested in cooking or the food industry. Leader: Mr Browne (S.Browne@hv.sch.je).

French GCSE (Edexcel) — four skills each 25%: Listening, Reading, Speaking, Writing. A native French speaker supports speaking prep. French is an official language of Jersey. Leader: Miss Durbano (r.durbano@hv.sch.je).

Geography (Edexcel IGCSE) — two exams: Physical Geography (1hr 10min) and Human Geography (1hr 45min). Includes real fieldwork. Good for students interested in the wider world. Leader: Mr Ingram (m.ingram@hv.sch.je).

History (Edexcel) is 100% exam across 3 papers — no coursework. Topics: Medicine in Britain, American West, Elizabethan England, Weimar & Nazi Germany. Reading and writing intensive. Leader: Mr Ingram (m.ingram@hv.sch.je).

ICT (Cambridge IGCSE) — 1 theory paper (40%) + 2 practical papers (60%). Covers software applications, spreadsheets, databases, and web design. Different from Computer Science — less programming, more using software. No named course leader in booklet.

Motor Vehicle Studies is a vocational course ideal for students interested in the automotive industry. Covers servicing, tools, health & safety, and tyres. Leads to a Level 2 diploma or apprenticeship. Counts as your ONE vocational choice. Leader: Miss Knowles (R.Knowles@hv.sch.je).

Music BTEC (Pearson International Level 2) — 50% internal projects, 50% externally assessed assignment. No traditional written exam. Graded like GCSE: Distinction* = 8.5, Distinction = 7, Merit = 5/6. Cannot choose both BTEC and GCSE Music. Leader: Miss Cooper (c.cooper@hv.sch.je).

Music GCSE (AQA) — Performing 30% + Composing 30% + Listening exam 40% (1hr 30min). You should already play an instrument or sing. Cannot choose both GCSE and BTEC Music. Leader: Miss Cooper (c.cooper@hv.sch.je).

Performing Arts BTEC (Edexcel) — 75% internal performance-based work, 25% external (video audition + application letter). No written exam. Options: Acting or Musical Theatre. Good for confident, creative team players. Leader: Mr Mortimer (d.mortimer@hv.sch.je).
Photography GCSE (AQA) — Portfolio 60% + 10-hour controlled exam piece 40%. Some written analysis required. Rewards creative thinking and visual skills. Leader: Mrs Boccone (s.boccone@hv.sch.je).

Psychology GCSE (OCR) — 100% exam, no coursework. Two 90-minute papers in Year 11. Topics include Criminal Psychology, Memory, Social Influence, Sleep & Dreaming. Includes maths questions and essays. Leader: Miss Halsall (a.halsall@hv.sch.je).

Religious Studies GCSE (AQA) — 100% exam, two papers (50% each). Covers Christianity, Islam, and 4 thematic modules including relationships, conflict, and crime. Must be chosen as Choice 1. Leader: Mrs Riley (c.riley@hv.sch.je).

Spanish GCSE (Edexcel) — four skills each 25%: Listening, Reading, Speaking, Writing. A native Spanish speaker supports speaking prep. Can be combined with Portuguese as a unique joint GCSE. Leader: Miss Durbano (r.durbano@hv.sch.je).

Combined Portuguese/Spanish GCSE — sit Portuguese GCSE in Year 9, then Spanish in Years 10-11. Ideal for students already fluent in Portuguese. Both assessed in 4 skills: Listening, Reading, Speaking, Writing. Leader: Miss Durbano (r.durbano@hv.sch.je).

Sport BTEC (Pearson) — mostly practical: 2 internal assignments + 1 external exam (1.5hrs). Covers sports analysis, coaching, fitness, and leadership. Good for students interested in PE or sports careers. Leader: Mrs Paine (p.paine@hv.sch.je).

Construction & Built Environment (Jersey Progression Award, Highlands College) — 2-year course. Year 9 school-based; Years 10-11 at Highlands one afternoon per week. Counts as your ONE vocational choice.
Hair & Beauty (Jersey Progression Award, Highlands College) — 2-year course. Year 9 school-based; Years 10-11 at Highlands one afternoon per week. Counts as your ONE vocational choice.

All students start Triple Science in Year 9 (Biology, Chemistry, Physics = 3 GCSEs). At end of Year 9, some may move to Combined Science (2 GCSEs). Six exams at end of Year 11. Core — not an option choice. Leader: Mrs Gallagher (r.gallagher@hv.sch.je).

IGCSE Mathematics (Edexcel) is compulsory for all students. Foundation tier (grades 1-5) or Higher tier (grades 4-9). Two calculator papers, assessed in November and May giving two chances to improve. Leader: Miss Hanrahan (m.hanrahan@hv.sch.je).

Both English subjects are compulsory. English Language: 50% exam, 50% coursework. English Literature: 60% exam, 40% coursework — texts include Of Mice and Men, An Inspector Calls, Macbeth. SPaG is only 5% of Literature marks. Leader: Mr Hann (t.hann@hv.sch.je).

Students with SEN may be eligible for access arrangements (extra time, reader, scribe, rest breaks, laptop). This is arranged through the school's SENCO based on evidence of need. Contact the school directly to discuss your child's specific requirements.

For dyslexic students, low-writing subjects like Art, Photography, and Performing Arts BTEC work well. English Literature notes SPaG is only 5% of marks. Access arrangements (extra time, scribe) are available in all subjects via the SENCO.

Subjects with clear structure and logical content suit many autistic students: Computer Science, History, Psychology, Business Studies. Art and DT offer self-directed project work with minimal social pressure. Speak to the SENCO about access arrangements.

Practical, project-based subjects often work well: Art, DT, Photography, Sport BTEC, Performing Arts. These offer varied tasks rather than long revision-heavy study. Speak to the SENCO about support arrangements in exams.
Haute Vallée School: 📞 01534 736524 | ✉️ enquiries@hv.sch.je | 🌐 www.hv.sch.je. Headteacher: Stuart J Hughes.

English and Maths are needed for almost every post-16 route. Universities value languages even if not continued to A Level. The EBacc route (humanities + language) is broadly respected. For specific career paths, speak to a school careers advisor.

Students take 3 option subjects plus 5 compulsory subjects (English Language, English Literature, Maths, and Triple or Combined Science). That's typically 8-9 GCSEs in total, depending on Science route.

For neurodivergent students, practical and project-based subjects often work best: Art, Photography, DT, Performing Arts BTEC, Sport BTEC. These avoid high-stakes exam pressure. Structured logical subjects like Computer Science and Psychology also suit many neurodivergent learners. Always speak to the SENCO about access arrangements.

Logical thinkers tend to do well in Computer Science (Python programming + theory), Maths-heavy subjects like Business Studies and Psychology (both include quantitative questions), and Geography (structured fieldwork and data analysis).

Subjects with little or no maths: Art, Photography, Performing Arts BTEC, Music (BTEC or GCSE), Food & Nutrition, Child Development, French, Spanish, History, Religious Studies, English Literature. Avoid Psychology (10%+ maths questions) and Computer Science (logic-heavy).

Maths-friendly options: Computer Science (logical, algorithm-based), Psychology (includes statistical analysis), Business Studies (financial calculations), Geography (data and statistics). Physics in Triple Science also rewards strong maths ability.

Creative and practical students are well served by: Art & Design, Photography, Design Technology, Performing Arts BTEC, Music BTEC, Food & Nutrition, and the Highlands vocational courses (Construction, Hair & Beauty, Motor Vehicle). All have heavy coursework or practical components.

Subjects to avoid if your child dislikes speaking or presenting: languages (French, Spanish — 25% speaking exam), Performing Arts. Better fits: Art, Photography, Computer Science, History, Geography, Psychology — all assessed through written work or practical projects with no performance element.

Strong readers and writers excel in History (essay-heavy, 100% exam), Religious Studies (analytical essays), English Literature (already compulsory), and Psychology (extended written answers). Geography also rewards good written communication.

Low or no exam subjects: Art & Design (no written exam), Photography (no written exam), Performing Arts BTEC (no written exam), Music BTEC (no written exam). Mixed coursework/exam: Child Development, DT, Food & Nutrition, Sport BTEC. Avoid History, Psychology, RS, Business — all 100% exam.

For science or medicine careers, Triple Science (already compulsory) is the priority. Good supporting options: Computer Science, Geography, Psychology, or Maths-heavy subjects like Business. Languages are also valued by medical schools. Confirm with a careers advisor for specific pathways.

For creative careers: Art & Design and Photography build a portfolio foundation. Design Technology suits product/industrial design paths. Performing Arts for theatre/media. These are best combined with at least one academic subject (History, Geography, a language) to keep future options open.

Computer Science (Choice 1) is the obvious pick. ICT is a softer alternative if programming feels too challenging. Business Studies complements a tech career well. Strong Maths (already compulsory) is essential for most tech routes at A Level and beyond.

Sport BTEC is the natural choice — practical, coaching and analysis focused. Food & Nutrition complements sport (nutrition science). Psychology is useful for sports psychology. PE is compulsory for all students regardless of options chosen.

HV offers French, Spanish, and Combined Portuguese/Spanish. Languages must be chosen as Choice 1. A language GCSE is highly valued by universities even if not continued to A Level. Jersey students have a real advantage with French as an official island language.

Start by identifying what your child enjoys and is good at — not what their friends choose. For Choice 1, most students pick a language, History, or Geography. Then balance the remaining two between practical and academic. The school also runs subject presentations during form time to help.

Subjects that are mostly or entirely exam-based: History (100% exam), Psychology (100% exam), Religious Studies (100% exam), Business Studies (100% exam), French/Spanish (exam + speaking). These suit students who prefer to revise and sit exams rather than manage ongoing projects.

Most demanding in terms of ongoing workload: Art and DT (large projects requiring time outside lessons), Music GCSE (regular practice needed), and the Highlands vocational courses (off-site commitment). History and Psychology are demanding in terms of content volume for exams.

For students who find school stressful, practical and creative subjects with less high-stakes exam pressure often help: Art, Photography, Performing Arts BTEC, Sport BTEC. These also build confidence and self-expression. Do speak to the school directly about pastoral support alongside options choices.

All GCSE subjects involve work outside lessons. Heaviest homework load: Art and DT (ongoing projects require significant time at home), Music GCSE (regular instrument practice), History and Psychology (substantial reading and revision). Lighter ongoing homework: BTEC subjects tend to be more classroom-based with projects completed in lessons.

The booklet does not mention specific out-of-school tutoring provision. For private tuition in Jersey, contact the school for recommendations, or search Jersey tutoring directories. Core subjects (Maths, English, Science) have the widest pool of available tutors on the island.

The booklet mentions enrichment sessions throughout Year 9 including PSHE. The 3-year GCSE model is specifically designed to allow time for extra-curricular activities alongside academic study. Contact the school for details of specific clubs or activities on offer.

The booklet highlights that the 3-year course gives additional time for thorough exam preparation. Specific revision support or intervention sessions are not detailed — contact the school directly to ask about what is available per subject.

The booklet encourages students to ask teachers questions and gather information before choosing. At home: help your child research subjects they are unsure about, visit open events, and avoid steering them based on your own preferences — choices should reflect their strengths and interests.

The booklet mentions subject leaders present to students during form time and lessons. For parent information events, contact the school directly — 01534 736524 or enquiries@hv.sch.je — as specific dates are not listed in the booklet.

The booklet encourages students to speak directly to subject leaders for each course. Each subject page lists the course leader and their email. For general options guidance, speak to your form tutor or Head of Year.
Choices are submitted at studentoptions.co/hautevallee using your child's unique login. You can log back in and amend choices before the deadline. If you have login issues, contact the school office.

A balanced combination typically mixes one essay/exam subject (History, Geography, RS, Psychology), one practical or creative subject (Art, DT, Photography, Performing Arts), and one language or technical subject (French, Spanish, Computer Science, Business). This keeps future pathways open and avoids overloading in any one direction.

IGCSE Maths and English Language are assessed in both November and May, giving students two opportunities to improve. For other subjects, resit options depend on the exam board. The 3-year course structure at HV is specifically designed to reduce the pressure of single-sitting exams.

GCSEs are graded 1-9 (9 being highest). BTECs are graded Pass/Merit/Distinction/Distinction* which convert to GCSE-equivalent grades (e.g. Distinction* = grade 8.5). The CeFE Financial Education award is a Level 2 qualification, equivalent to a GCSE pass.

The booklet specifically advises against choosing subjects based on what friends are doing. Option blocks are formed after all choices are submitted, so there's no guarantee of being in the same class as friends anyway. Choices should be based on your child's own strengths and interests.

Both are 100% practical with no written exam (portfolio 60% + exam piece 40%). Art uses traditional and experimental media; Photography is digital/lens-based with some written analysis. Choose based on whether your child prefers making physical artwork or working with images and cameras.

keywords: difference between computer science and ict
which is better cs or ict
responses.response: Computer Science involves programming in Python and computational thinking — more demanding. ICT focuses on using software (spreadsheets, databases, web design) — more accessible. CS requires Choice 1 slot; ICT does not. CS is better for tech careers; ICT for general digital skills.

History is 100% exam, essay-heavy and reading intensive. Geography has two exams but includes fieldwork and data analysis. Both count for EBacc and must be Choice 1. Geography suits analytical thinkers; History suits strong writers.

keywords: should i do french or spanish
responses.response: Both assessed identically — four skills at 25% each. French is an official language of Jersey with deep cultural roots on the island. Spanish is the world's second most spoken language. Choose based on your child's existing knowledge and enjoyment.

Music GCSE requires strong instrument/vocal ability and includes a listening exam. Music BTEC is more project and production focused with no written exam — better for students interested in the music industry broadly rather than classical performance.

Both are 100% exam with no coursework. Business is more numerical and real-world focused; Psychology is essay and science-based with some maths. Business suits entrepreneurial students; Psychology suits those interested in people and behaviour.

There is no GCSE PE at HV — the option is Sport BTEC. This covers coaching, sports analysis and fitness. Core PE is compulsory for all students anyway. The BTEC suits students interested in sports leadership or coaching.

Both have 50% coursework and 50% exam. DT focuses on designing and making products with materials. Food and Nutrition focuses on cooking and nutrition science. Very different in practice — choose based on your child's genuine interest.

Performing Arts BTEC is team-based theatre and musical theatre performance. Music is about composing, performing an instrument, and understanding music theory. Performing Arts suits confident performers; Music suits students with an instrument or strong interest in music production.

Triple Science already gives 3 GCSEs so your options sit alongside it. A good balance: one humanities/language (Choice 1), one practical/creative, one technical. Computer Science pairs very well with Triple Science for STEM pathways.

To achieve the full EBacc at HV: English and Maths (compulsory), Science (compulsory), plus a language AND a humanity from your 3 options. This leaves one free choice for a creative or practical subject.

Choosing multiple creative subjects is possible but consider balancing with one academic subject. Universities and colleges generally like to see some written subjects alongside creative ones — History, Geography, or Psychology work well.

Three 100% exam subjects creates a very heavy revision load in Year 11. This suits highly academic students but can be stressful. Consider whether one practical or coursework subject would provide a better balance.

High-ability students often thrive in History, Psychology, Computer Science, and languages — subjects with clear grade criteria and stretching content. The 3-year course at HV allows time to reach higher-level content, a real advantage for ambitious students.

Hands-on learners are well served by: Design Technology, Food and Nutrition, Art, Construction or Motor Vehicle (Highlands), and Sport BTEC. All have significant practical components assessed over time rather than in a single exam.

Visual learners often excel in Art, Photography, Geography (maps, diagrams, fieldwork), Design Technology, and Computer Science (flowcharts and diagrams). These subjects reward visual thinking and spatial awareness.

Collaborative subjects: Performing Arts BTEC (group performances), Music BTEC (ensemble work), Sport BTEC (team coaching). Languages also involve pair and group speaking practice. Most other GCSEs are primarily individual work.

Self-directed learners do well in Art (personal portfolio), Photography (own projects), Computer Science (solo programming), and History (independent research and essay writing). These reward students who can manage their own time.

Subjects that build confidence gradually: Art, Photography, Performing Arts BTEC, and Sport BTEC assess over time rather than in one exam. Encourage choosing subjects they enjoy — enjoyment usually leads to better outcomes than choosing what seems impressive.

Perfectionists often do well in History, Psychology, Computer Science, and Art — all reward thoroughness and attention to detail. The main risk is spending too long on coursework, so time management is important.

HV is one of Jersey's secondary schools offering GCSEs from Year 8 — earlier than most UK schools. Jersey's bilingual heritage makes French particularly relevant. The Highlands College partnership offers vocational routes unique to the island. 2024 results were described as outstanding.

Jersey's main industries are financial services, hospitality, tourism, agriculture, and construction. Business Studies and Financial Education (CeFE) suit finance paths. Computer Science for tech. Food and Nutrition and Construction (Highlands) directly address local industry needs.

Highlands College partnership courses (Construction, Hair and Beauty) run over 2 years — Year 9 school-based, Years 10-11 one afternoon per week at Highlands. These are Jersey Progression Awards valued by local employers. You can only choose ONE Highlands course.

Jersey has a large Portuguese-speaking community. HV offers a unique Combined Portuguese/Spanish GCSE — ideal for students already fluent in Portuguese. They gain two language GCSEs: Portuguese in Year 9, then Spanish in Years 10-11. A significant advantage for bilingual students.

Access arrangements (extra time, reader, scribe, rest breaks, laptop) apply to all GCSE exams and are arranged through the school SENCO. Evidence of need is required — speak to the SENCO as early as possible, ideally before Year 9.

Most GCSE exams are sat at the end of Year 11. IGCSE Maths and English Language have additional sittings in November and May, allowing students to improve earlier grades. Some subjects may allow early entry where appropriate.
Coursework deadlines are set by individual subject teachers and vary by subject and year. Contact subject leaders directly — their email addresses are listed on each subject page of the booklet.

Predicted grades are set by subject teachers based on classwork, assessments, and mocks throughout the course. The 3-year model gives teachers more time to assess accurately. Contact your child's subject teacher directly for grade expectations.

Before the deadline, choices can be changed via the online platform. After the deadline, speak to the school — changes may be possible early in Year 9. Choices based on genuine interest and strength rarely lead to regret.

The booklet is clear: choices should not be based on what parents want. Students who choose subjects they enjoy consistently outperform those pushed into subjects they dislike. The best parental role is to help research options, not direct the decision.

To keep options open: choose at least one humanity (History, Geography, RS) and consider a language. The EBacc route is safest for keeping A Level and university options wide. Avoid choosing three very niche subjects if the future direction is uncertain.

The booklet does not publish popularity data. History, Geography, Computer Science, and Art are broadly popular at most schools. The school runs subject presentations so students can gauge interest. Popularity should not influence choice — timetable blocks are built around demand.

Class sizes are not specified in the options booklet. Vocational Highlands courses tend to be smaller groups. For specific class size information, contact the school on 01534 736524 or enquiries@hv.sch.je.
Some subjects require minimum numbers to run. Reserve choices are collected for exactly this reason. The school aims to accommodate as many first choices as possible when building option blocks.

If your child is new to HV, contact the school office directly on 01534 736524 or enquiries@hv.sch.je. The options coordinator will advise on the process for late or new joiners as the standard deadline may not apply.

For EAL students, practical subjects with less written demand work well: Art, Photography, DT, Performing Arts, Sport BTEC, and Highlands vocational courses. Languages like Spanish or Portuguese/Spanish may also leverage existing skills. Speak to the SENCO about EAL support available.

The 3-year course allows exploration of higher-level content. HV can enter students for exams early if teachers believe it is beneficial. For exceptionally able students, speak to the school about stretch opportunities — particularly in Maths, Science, and Computer Science.

For students with significant attendance difficulties, exam-based subjects (History, Psychology, RS, Business) may be more manageable as content can be caught up at home. Coursework subjects require consistent in-school time. Speak to the SENCO and pastoral team about the best approach.

Bilingual students have a real advantage in language GCSEs. The Combined Portuguese/Spanish option is specifically designed for Portuguese-speaking students. If fluent in French or Spanish, speak to Miss Durbano about appropriate challenge level.

The booklet does not detail subject costs. Some practical subjects (Art, DT, Food and Nutrition) may require materials. Highlands courses may have associated costs. Contact the school directly for information on subject-specific costs or financial support available.

Current subject teachers are the best source of advice on GCSE suitability. The booklet encourages students to speak directly to subject leaders. If a teacher has recommended for or against a subject, that guidance is worth taking seriously.

Feeling anxious about options is very common. Reassurances: reserve choices exist as a safety net, the school builds blocks to maximise first choices, and early Year 9 changes are sometimes possible. Focus on what your child enjoys and is good at — that is genuinely the best guide.

Religious Studies GCSE (AQA) covers Christianity and Islam alongside philosophical and ethical themes. It is open to students of all faiths and none — it is an academic subject examining beliefs and ethics, not faith formation. It must be chosen as Choice 1 if selected.

The booklet does not indicate competitive entry for any subject. Option blocks are formed based on overall demand. Reserve choices ensure students have a fallback if their preferred subject does not run or blocks do not work out.

keywords: how can i help at home
responses.response: The booklet encourages students to research subjects and ask teachers questions before choosing. At home, help your child explore subject content online, watch related documentaries, and visit subject-specific websites. The most important thing is that choices feel right to them, not to you.

GCSE exams are mainly in May and June of Year 11. IGCSE Maths and English Language also have a November sitting, giving students a second chance to improve. Exact dates are published by exam boards (Edexcel, AQA, OCR, Cambridge) each year — the school will share the full timetable closer to the time.

HV specifically mentions that the 3-year course allows flexibility to enter students for exams when teachers believe it will benefit them — not just at the end of Year 11. IGCSE Maths and English Language can be sat in November of Year 11. Speak to the relevant subject teacher about early entry possibilities.

Study leave arrangements are not detailed in the options booklet — these are set by the school annually. Typically Jersey secondary schools offer study leave during the main exam period in May/June of Year 11. Contact the school directly for the current study leave policy: 01534 736524 or enquiries@hv.sch.je.

Mock exams are mentioned for several subjects — languages (French, Spanish) have mock Listening, Reading and Writing at end of Year 10 and twice in Year 11. Other subjects will have internal mocks set by the school. Exact mock timetables are set annually — the school will communicate these to students and parents.

GCSE results are typically released in late August — Results Day is usually the third Thursday of August. For the Class of 2027 (current Year 8), results would be August 2027. The school contacts students directly with results and supports them with next steps.

The booklet highlights that the 3-year course gives significantly more time for thorough exam preparation compared to traditional 2-year courses. Subject teachers will advise on revision strategies specific to each course. General advice: start early, use past papers, and work consistently rather than cramming.

HV uses multiple exam boards: Edexcel/Pearson for Maths, Business, Computer Science, DT, French, Spanish, Geography, History, Sport BTEC, Performing Arts; AQA for Art, Food, Music GCSE, Photography, RS; OCR/Cambridge for English, Science, Psychology, Child Development, ICT. The exam board is listed on each subject page of the booklet.

IGCSE English Language and IGCSE Mathematics both have November sittings in Year 11, in addition to the main May sitting. This gives students two opportunities to achieve their best grade. Other subjects do not have November sittings.
